reasoning: The most recent 3-month T-bill yield (Mar 13) is 3.61%, which is the highest it has been in the last month. The FOMC meeting is set for Mar 17-18, falling between the baseline date (Mar 15) and resolution date (Mar 22). CME FedWatch indicates a 96% probability of the Fed holding rates, with a 4% chance of a 25 bps cut, and 0% probability of a rate hike. This introduces an asymmetric downside risk to yields. Furthermore, because a hold is fully priced in, and subsequent meetings (May/June) have higher cut probabilities, as the 3-month window rolls forward into June, the T-bill yield will naturally embed more rate cut expectations, causing a gentle downward drift. Given the discrete reporting (basis points), for the variable to strictly *increase* it must reach 3.62%, which is highly unlikely given the current local maximum and asymmetric downward risks.
